attributes.stem:
A two-month-old girl presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine check. Her parent is concerned about a large dark brown patch on her back that has been present since birth. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her immunisations are up to date. She is meeting all her developmental milestones appropriately.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 25 cm dark brown plaque with irregular borders and varying pigmentation covering most of her back. There are several smaller satellite lesions nearby. The surface is slightly raised with some hair growth.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Regular monitoring with photography and staged excision
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a large congenital melanocytic naevus (CMN). CMNs are classified by size, with those >20 cm considered 'giant'. These lesions carry a significant risk of malignant transformation (approximately 5 -23% lifetime risk for giant CMN) and can also be associated with neurocutaneous melanosis when located on the posterior axis. Congenital melanocytic naevus are common - small congenital naevi of less than 1.5cm occurs in 1:100 live births and giant CMN occur in 1: 20 000 live births. It often appears as flat and tan lesions that can evolve to be raised, hypertrichosis (hairy) or nodular as the patient ages.

Regular monitoring with photography and staged excision is the most appropriate management plan. This approach allows for documentation of changes while planning surgical removal in stages. Early intervention (typically starting between 6-12 months of age) is preferred as the skin is more elastic in infancy, and removal before school age can reduce psychosocial impact. Screening can also be done with MRI Brain and spine if required.

Reassurance and review at puberty would be inappropriate given the size of the lesion and its associated melanoma risk. Delaying intervention could increase both medical and psychological complications.

Immediate complete excision would be technically challenging and potentially dangerous in an infant this young. The size of the lesion would require extensive reconstruction, making a staged approach more appropriate.

Laser therapy alone is not effective for large CMNs as it does not address the deeper components of the naevus and does not reduce the risk of malignant transformation even with removal.

Dermabrasion is similarly ineffective as it only removes superficial components of the naevus and does not reduce malignant potential.

Management of large CMNs requires a multidisciplinary approach involving dermatologists, plastic surgeons, and sometimes neurologists (if central nervous system involvement is suspected). The timing and extent of surgical intervention must be balanced against the risks of general anaesthesia in young children and the technical challenges of reconstruction. Patients must also adhere to strict sun protection.
learningPoints: Large congenital melanocytic naevi (>20 cm) have a higher risk of malignant transformation and require regular monitoring.
Serial photography is essential for monitoring changes in congenital melanocytic naevi.
Prophylactic excision of large congenital melanocytic naevi should be considered in early childhood to reduce melanoma risk.
references: Oakley A, Raj G. Congenital Melanocytic Naevus. DermNet NZ. 2024. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/congenital-melanocytic-naevi
Mologousis MA, Tsai SY-C, Tissera KA, Levin YS, Hawryluk EB. Updates in the Management of Congenital Melanocytic Nevi. Children. 2024; 11(1):62.  Available at: https://www.mdpi.com/2227-9067/11/1/62
